text: The video shows a puckered area in the sigmoid colon, identified as colon cancer, with visible movement back and forth.
A five French open-ended catheter is inserted into the right ureter, and five milliliters of IC green dye are injected to enhance visualization of the ureter during the operation.
The surgeon lateralizes the sigmoid colon, revealing the ureter highlighted in green under infrared light, which aids in its identification during the procedure.

The surgical team utilizes two robotic arms and two non-robotic laparoscopic ports for the procedure. The surgeon uses a bipolar device to desiccate a venous branch, demonstrating the technique for managing small vessels.
The surgeon prepares to dissect around the inferior mesenteric artery to ensure adequate lymph node sampling for cancer staging, using either robotic or handheld instruments.

The surgeon completes the dissection of the mesentery, revealing the inferior mesenteric artery. Due to its size, the artery is clipped rather than desiccated. The specimen is removed, showing enough redundant colon for a potential side-to-side anastomosis. The splenic flexure was mobilized initially.

The surgeon performs a hand-sewn intracorporeal anastomosis on the small bowel, focusing on the posterior serosal layer of the two-layered anastomosis. The lesion is positioned approximately twenty-six centimeters from the anal verge, allowing for an adequate margin due to the mobilization and redundancy of the colon.
The surgeon begins suturing the mucosal layer of the anastomosis using a running technique with 3-0 Vicryl on an SH needle. Canal stitches are applied to secure the ends and edges of the anastomosis.

The surgeon demonstrates the canal suture technique on the corners of the anastomosis, using an outside-in and inside-out pattern to ensure even coaptation. The running suture is applied over the linear portion once the corners are completed, similar to the technique used in open hand-sewn procedures.
